svg.title: Joint Probability Density: Boson vs. Fermion vs. Distinguishable
svg.desc: Three heat-map panels showing |psi(x1,x2)|^2 for two particles (psi_1, psi_2) in a 1D box. Boson (symmetric): density enhanced on diagonal x1=x2. Fermion (antisymmetric): density zero on diagonal (Pauli node). Distinguishable: off-diagonal blobs, no diagonal feature. Marginal densities below are identical for all three.

cajal:figure.book: Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom
cajal:figure.figure-title: Joint Probability Density: Boson vs. Fermion vs. Distinguishable
cajal:figure.author: Humanitarians AI Incorporated

svg.text: Particles in orbitals ψ1, ψ2 of 1D infinite well. Diagonal line: x1=x2.
Marginal density ρ(x1) — identical for all three symmetry classes
The single-particle marginal density is exchange-statistics blind — it is the same for bosons,
fermions, and distinguishable particles. Only the joint (two-particle) correlations differ.
Gold diagonal: x1=x2 line. |ψ-|2 has a node there; |ψ+|2 has a peak.
